title: Effects of Resistance Training Frequency on Measures of Muscle Hypertrophy: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
population: trained and untrained adults across 10 studies
intervention: Comparison of training frequency (1× vs 2×+ per muscle per week) with volume equated.
key_finding: When weekly volume is equated, 2+ sessions per muscle per week produces greater hypertrophy than 1 session.
practical_takeaway: Train each muscle ≥ 2× per week as a default. 1×/week works for low-volume programs but is suboptimal when total weekly sets are matched.

title: Dose-response relationship between weekly resistance training volume and increases in muscle mass: A systematic review and meta-analysis
intervention: Per-muscle weekly hard set count vs hypertrophy outcomes.
key_finding: Higher weekly set volume produced greater hypertrophy in a dose-dependent fashion across the studied range (~5–20+ sets).
practical_takeaway: More volume = more growth, up to a fatigue ceiling. Most trained lifters benefit from 10–20 hard sets per muscle per week. Beginners can grow on as few as 4–10.

title: Strength and Hypertrophy Adaptations Between Low- vs. High-Load Resistance Training: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
journal: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research
intervention: Low-load (≤60% 1RM) vs high-load (>60% 1RM) training to volitional failure.
key_finding: Hypertrophy is similar between low- and high-load training when sets are taken close to failure. Strength favors heavier loads.
practical_takeaway: Rep range for hypertrophy is broad: 5–30 reps work when sets are close to failure (RIR ≤ 3). Use heavier loads (≤ 6 reps) when strength is also a goal.

title: Effects of resistance training performed to failure or non-failure on muscular strength and hypertrophy: A systematic review and meta-analysis
journal: Journal of Sport and Health Science
intervention: Sets taken to volitional failure vs stopped 1–4 reps short.
key_finding: Training to failure does not produce more hypertrophy than stopping 1–3 reps short when volume is equated. Strength gains are similar or slightly favor non-failure.
practical_takeaway: Train at RIR 0–3 for hypertrophy. Reserve true failure for the last set of isolation movements. Going to failure on every compound set increases fatigue without adding growth.

title: Effect of Range of Motion on Muscle Strength and Thickness Adaptations: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis
population: trained and untrained lifters across 6 studies
intervention: Full range of motion vs partial range of motion training.
key_finding: Full ROM produces greater hypertrophy than partial ROM, especially when the lengthened position is loaded.
practical_takeaway: Train through a full range of motion. Lengthened-position loading (deep squat, stretched-position rows) is particularly effective.

title: Loaded Inter-set Stretching Augments Resistance Training Hypertrophy
intervention: Standard sets vs sets with passive loaded stretching between sets.
key_finding: Loaded stretching of the trained muscle between sets augmented hypertrophy.
practical_takeaway: Lengthened-position training is a real lever. Exercises that load the stretched portion (deep squats, dumbbell flyes, pullovers) likely outperform their shortened counterparts.

title: Resistance Training With Single vs. Multiple Sets for Strength: A Meta-analysis
journal: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research
intervention: Single-set vs multi-set protocols on 1RM strength.
key_finding: Multi-set training produces ~46% greater strength gains than single-set training.
practical_takeaway: Single sets are better than nothing but multi-set work is meaningfully superior for strength. ≥ 3 sets per exercise is a sensible default.

title: Single vs. multiple sets of resistance exercise for muscle hypertrophy: a meta-analysis
journal: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research
intervention: Single-set vs multi-set on muscle thickness.
key_finding: Multi-set produces ~40% greater hypertrophy than single-set.
practical_takeaway: Multi-set work is required for non-trivial hypertrophy. 3+ sets per exercise is the floor for trained lifters.

title: Resistance Training Volume Does Not Influence Lean Body Mass in Highly Trained Lifters Following a Hypertrophy-Style Routine
intervention: Moderate vs high weekly volume in trained populations.
key_finding: Beyond a per-muscle threshold, additional sets did not produce additional hypertrophy in highly trained lifters.
practical_takeaway: There is a per-muscle volume ceiling. Adding sets past your MAV mostly adds fatigue, not growth. Trained lifters should build progressively, not aggressively.

title: Greater Hypertrophy with Higher vs. Lower Set Volumes Across Different Muscles
journal: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research
intervention: 5 vs 10 vs 15+ sets per muscle per session weekly.
key_finding: Hypertrophy increased with volume in a partly muscle-specific way; some muscles plateau earlier than others.
practical_takeaway: Don't apply a single weekly set count across all muscles. Calves, side delts, and biceps tolerate more volume than chest or quads. Tune per muscle based on response.

title: Resistance Training-Induced Changes in Strength and Specific Force at the Fiber and Whole Muscle Level
intervention: Mechanistic study of muscle fiber response to resistance training.
key_finding: Edema accounts for early-program 'gains'; true hypertrophy emerges after ~3 weeks of consistent training.
practical_takeaway: First 2–3 weeks of a new program inflate visible size with edema. Don't judge a program until you've been on it 6+ weeks.

title: Effect of resistance training frequency on gains in muscular strength: a systematic review and meta-analysis
intervention: Training frequency (1×, 2×, 3×+/week) for strength outcomes.
key_finding: Strength gains scale slightly with frequency when volume is equated; effect is modest.
practical_takeaway: For strength, 2–3× per week per lift is a good default. Higher frequency helps if recovery permits but isn't required.

title: Effects of resistance training to muscle failure on acute and longitudinal muscle hypertrophy
intervention: Sets to failure vs stopped 1–3 reps short.
key_finding: Training to failure does not augment hypertrophy beyond ~2–3 RIR; recovery is impaired with consistent failure.
practical_takeaway: Stop most working sets at RIR 1–3. Hitting failure on every set burns recovery without buying growth.

title: Eccentric Resistance Training Versus Concentric Resistance Training for Strength: a Meta-Analysis
intervention: Eccentric-only vs concentric-only training.
key_finding: Eccentric training produces slightly greater strength and hypertrophy than concentric-only.
practical_takeaway: Don't drop the eccentric. Control the lowering phase (~2s); accentuating eccentrics is a niche tool, not a default.

title: Longer Inter-set Rest Periods Promote Greater Muscle Strength and Hypertrophy
journal: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research
intervention: 1-min vs 3-min rest between sets.
key_finding: 3-min rest produced greater strength and hypertrophy than 1-min rest.
practical_takeaway: Rest 2–3 min between compound sets. Short rests don't save time enough to compensate for the lost stimulus.

title: A Systematic Review, Meta-Analysis and Meta-Regression of the Effect of Protein Supplementation on Resistance Training-Induced Gains in Muscle Mass and Strength
population: trained and untrained adults across 49 studies
intervention: Protein supplementation alongside resistance training.
key_finding: Optimal protein intake for hypertrophy is ~1.6 g/kg bodyweight/day; benefits plateau around 2.2 g/kg.
practical_takeaway: Aim for 1.6–2.2 g protein per kg bodyweight daily. Distribute across 3–5 meals. More than 2.2 g/kg is unlikely to help.

title: Sleep and Muscle Recovery: Endocrinological and Molecular Basis for a New and Promising Hypothesis
intervention: n/a
key_finding: Sleep deprivation reduces protein synthesis and impairs anabolic hormonal milieu.
practical_takeaway: Sleep is the highest-leverage recovery tool. < 6h chronically erodes hypertrophy and strength gains. Prioritize 7–9h before training optimizations.

title: Resistance Training-Induced Strength Gains Are Maintained for at Least 4 Weeks After Detraining
journal: Scandinavian Journal of Medicine & Science in Sports
intervention: Strength loss across detraining periods.
key_finding: Strength is largely preserved across 2–4 weeks of detraining; significant losses begin around week 4.
practical_takeaway: Skipping a week off — illness, travel, life — won't undo your training. Don't panic-train through a deload.

title: Concurrent training: a meta-analysis examining interference of aerobic and resistance exercises
journal: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research
population: trained and untrained adults across 21 studies
intervention: Combined endurance and resistance training vs resistance-only.
key_finding: Concurrent training attenuates strength and hypertrophy when running is the endurance modality and lower-body lifting overlaps.
practical_takeaway: If hypertrophy is the priority, separate lower-body lifts and lower-body conditioning by 6+ hours. Cycling interferes less than running. Upper-body conditioning interferes minimally.

title: Effects of Different Energy Restriction Protocols on Body Composition in Trained and Untrained Individuals
journal: Journal of the International Society of Sports Nutrition
intervention: Various deficit protocols during contest prep.
key_finding: Deficits of 0.5–1% of bodyweight per week preserve lean mass; faster losses sacrifice strength and muscle.
practical_takeaway: For fat loss while preserving muscle, target 0.5–1% bodyweight per week. Aggressive cuts cost lean mass and performance.

title: Higher protein intakes preserve muscle and strength during energy restriction in resistance-trained men
intervention: 1.2 vs 2.4 g/kg protein during a 40% deficit with resistance training.
key_finding: High-protein group gained lean mass and lost more fat in a deficit; low-protein lost some lean mass.
practical_takeaway: In a deficit, push protein toward 2.0–2.4 g/kg. It's worth the calories.

title: Polarized Training Has Greater Impact on Key Endurance Variables Than Threshold, High Intensity, or High Volume Training
intervention: Polarized (80% Z2 / 20% high-intensity) vs other distributions.
key_finding: Polarized distribution improves VO2max and time-to-exhaustion more than threshold or pyramidal models.
practical_takeaway: For conditioning: ~80% easy / ~20% hard. Add 1–2× weekly VO2max intervals (3–8 min) at high intensity, plus zone 2 base work.

title: Daily Undulating Periodization vs. Linear Periodization for Strength: A Meta-Analysis
intervention: DUP vs linear periodization for strength.
key_finding: Differences are small; both work. DUP slightly favored in some intermediate populations.
practical_takeaway: Beginners: linear progression. Intermediates: DUP or block. Pick the one you'll execute consistently — adherence beats theoretical optimality.

title: Specificity of Resistance Training Adaptations: Effects of Power-Lifting and Bodybuilding Style Training on Strength and Hypertrophy
intervention: High-volume hypertrophy vs high-intensity strength training.
key_finding: Hypertrophy block produced more growth; strength block produced more 1RM gain.
practical_takeaway: If you want size, train like a bodybuilder. If you want a 1RM, train like a powerlifter. Hybrid programs split the difference.

title: Effects of resistance training repetition velocity on strength and hypertrophy
intervention: Slow vs fast intentional concentric velocities.
key_finding: When intent to move fast is matched, concentric velocity has minor impact on hypertrophy.
practical_takeaway: Tempo doesn't need micromanaging. Control the eccentric (~2s), drive the concentric with intent. Don't slow-grind reps for no reason.

title: Creatine supplementation and resistance training in resistance-trained men: A meta-analysis
journal: Journal of the International Society of Sports Nutrition
intervention: Creatine monohydrate supplementation.
key_finding: Creatine modestly increases lean mass, strength, and short-duration power output.
practical_takeaway: Creatine monohydrate, 3–5 g/day, no loading needed. Cheapest, best-evidenced ergogenic for lifters.

title: Caffeine and Strength: A Meta-Analysis
journal: Journal of the International Society of Sports Nutrition
intervention: Caffeine 3–6 mg/kg vs placebo before training.
key_finding: Caffeine produces small but reliable improvements in strength and power.
practical_takeaway: Caffeine 3–6 mg/kg ~30–45 min pre-workout improves performance. Don't stack daily — tolerance erodes the effect.

title: Stretching after resistance training does not enhance recovery
intervention: Static stretching post-workout for recovery.
key_finding: Post-training static stretching does not meaningfully accelerate recovery or reduce DOMS.
practical_takeaway: Stretching has its place (mobility, sport-specific ROM) but it's not a recovery tool. Don't blame skipped stretching for soreness.

title: Female Resistance Training: Acute and Chronic Hormonal Responses
intervention: Review of sex-specific training responses.
key_finding: Hypertrophy training principles transfer between sexes. Women may tolerate slightly more volume and recover faster between sets.
practical_takeaway: Programming principles are largely sex-neutral. Women may benefit from slightly higher volume and shorter inter-set rest, but the same rep ranges and frequency apply.

title: Resistance Training in Older Adults: A Meta-Analysis on Muscle Mass and Strength
intervention: Various resistance training protocols.
key_finding: Older adults respond robustly to resistance training; moderate-to-high loads with sufficient volume are most effective.
practical_takeaway: Age does not preclude muscle gain. Older lifters need the same fundamentals — moderate-to-heavy loads, multiple sets, progressive overload — but may benefit from longer recovery between hard sessions.
